476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]

492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}

497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,

502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561

563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok
682	[Lowest-degree cokernel description across an acyclic short exact sequence]
683	  \label{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}
684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}
685	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
686	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
687	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
688	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
689	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
690	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
691	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
692	  % between abelian categories and assume
693	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
694	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
695	  % of $\mathcal{A}$."
696	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "As $RF$ is an exact functor and since
697	  % $R^iF = 0$ for $i < 0$ and $R^0F = F$
698	  % (Lemma \ref{lemma-left-exact-higher-derived})
699	  % we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
700	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
701	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
702	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
705	  \[
706	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
707	  \]
708	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
709	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
710	  \[
711	    (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
712	  \]
713	  i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by the
714	  surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
715	\end{lemma}
716	
717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}

750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

21	\section{Setup and motivation}
22	
23	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
24	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
25	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
26	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
27	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
28	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
29	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
30	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
31	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
32	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
33	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
34	
35	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
36	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
37	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
38	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
39	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
40	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
41	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
42	\[
43	  \begin{array}{ccc}
44	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
45	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
46	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
47	  \end{array}
48	\]
49	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
50	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
51	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
52	
53	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}

77	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
78	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write

80	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
81	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
82	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
83	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
84	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
85	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
86	  \[
87	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
88	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
89	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
90	  \]
91	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
92	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
93	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
94	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
95	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
96	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
97	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
98	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
99	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
100	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
101	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
102	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
103	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
104	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
105	\end{definition}

171	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
172	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
173	
174	It is convenient to factor the construction of
175	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
176	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
177	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
178	
179	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
180	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
181	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
182	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
183	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
184	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
185	\[
186	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
187	    \;=\;
188	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
189	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
190	\]
191	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
192	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
193	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
194	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
195	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
196	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
197	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
198	
199	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
200	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
201	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
202	\[
203	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
204	  \qquad
205	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
206	\]
207	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
208	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
209	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
210	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
211	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
212	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
213	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
214	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
215	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
216	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
217	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
218	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
219	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
220	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
222	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
223	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
224	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
225	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
226	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
227	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
228	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
229	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
230	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
231	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
232	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
233	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
234	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
235	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
236	
237	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
238	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
239	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
240	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
241	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
242	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
243	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
244	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
245	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
246	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
247	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
248	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
249	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
250	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
251	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
252	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
253	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
254	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
255	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
256	
257	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
258	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
259	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
260	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional

content:
Let me now read the STRATEGY.md and some more of the chapter, particularly the section around the keystone proof (lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule at line 4294). I've already read lines 4120-4419. Now I need to understand the rest of the keystone proof fully and then look at the final theorem.

The key thing I need to focus on in the directive is:
1. The keystone `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` - I've just read the full statement and proof, lines 4293-4369. Let me analyze it carefully.
2. The proof strategy uses a finite standard cover to reduce to the localized case, then applies a presentation argument on each basic open set to establish the localized module property.
3. The key step "under the section comparison" asserts that the localized section map satisfies the required property, but this may be hiding an implicit sheaf-gluing isomorphism `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` that needs scrutiny.
4. The descent then follows from applying the covering lemma to combine these local results.

Now I'm examining whether `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` actually establishes the claim about `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` directly, or if it relies on an unstated sheaf-theoretic comparison that should be made explicit. ≅ Γ(D(f · g_j), F)` — the localized sections over D(f) match the sections over the smaller open set D(fg_j).

The proof invokes the section comparison lemma to argue that the g_j-localized map (ρ_f)_{g_j} satisfies the IsLocalizedModule property for powers of f. But the cited lemma only gives the definitional equality of restriction for opens in Spec R_{g_j}, so I need to trace through how this connects to the localization property of (ρ_f)_{g_j} itself.

Now I have a clear picture of the key issue. Let me trace through the logic:

1. **`lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation`** (line 4072): For F with a global presentation on Spec R, `Γ(Spec R, F) → Γ(D(f), F)` is IsLocalizedModule(powers f).
   - This is fine for F_{(g_j)} on Spec R_{g_j}.
   - But wait - it applies to F_{(g_j)} on Spec R_{g_j}: gives `Γ(Spec R_{g_j}, F_{(g_j)}) → Γ(D(f_in_R_{g_j}), F_{(g_j)})` is IsLocalizedModule.
   - By `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib`, the global sections of F_{(g_j)} on Spec R_{g_j} equal the global sections of F on D(g_j).

2. **`lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover`** (line 4787): The span-cover descent takes a map `g : M → N` between R-modules, and the hypothesis that for each j, the localization `g_{s_j} : M_{s_j} → N_{s_j}` is IsLocalizedModule(powers f).

3. The conclusion is that `g` itself is IsLocalizedModule(powers f). When applied to `ρ_f : Γ(X,F) → Γ(D(f),F)`, I need to verify that each localization `(ρ_f)_{g_j} : Γ(X,F)_{g_j} → Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}` satisfies this property.

4. However, what `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` actually gives me about F_{(g_j)} is that `Γ(D(g_j), F) → Γ(D(fg_j), F)` is IsLocalizedModule(powers f), which isn't quite the same as what I need for the localization at g_j.

5. To bridge this gap, I need to establish isomorphisms: `Γ(D(g_j), F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` and `Γ(D(fg_j), F) ≅ Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}`, ensuring these are compatible with the underlying maps.

6. Both isomorphisms should follow from applying `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` directly—but that creates a circular dependency. Let me reconsider the structure: `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` descends a map `g : M → N` from its localized versions `g_{s_j}`, so I'm setting M = Γ(X, F), N = Γ(D(f), F), and g = ρ_f, where each localized map `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` needs to satisfy IsLocalizedModule(powers f). The key insight is that `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` applied to F_{(g_j)} should provide what I need. = Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}`.

Now I'm hitting a circularity issue: the identification `Γ(D(g_j), F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` is exactly what the lemma I'm trying to prove should give me for g_j. For the other direction, I need to show `Γ(D(fg_j), F) ≅ Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}`, which uses the fact that `D(fg_j) = D(f) ∩ D(g_j)` and localizing sections of F on D(f) at g_j.

Let me reconsider the approach. The localization map `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` is obtained by localizing the restriction map `ρ_f : Γ(X,F) → Γ(D(f),F)` at g_j, and I need to verify this satisfies the IsLocalizedModule property for powers of f. The presentation lemma gives me that the section-restriction map from `Γ(Spec R_{g_j}, F_{(g_j)})` to `Γ(D_{{R_{g_j}}}(f), F_{(g_j)})` has this property, so the real question is whether these two maps can be identified.

The section comparison lemma relates sections on the restricted sheaf to sections on the original sheaf via the open immersion `ι : D(g_j) → Spec R`, so sections of `F_{(g_j)}` over an open V correspond to sections of F over the image under ι.

This means `Γ(Spec R_{g_j}, F_{(g_j)})` equals `Γ(D(g_j), F)` when we identify the spectra appropriately, and the distinguished open `D_{R_{g_j}}(f)` in the localized ring corresponds to `D(fg_j)` back in Spec R.

Applying the localized module lemma to `F_{(g_j)}` gives a map from sections over `D(g_j)` to sections over `D(fg_j)`, which should be a localization at powers of the image of f in the localized ring `R_{g_j}`.

Now I need to connect this to the descent argument by feeding the localized map `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` into the spanning cover lemma to complete the verification. The three identifications need to commute in a triangle, but the proof is circular because it relies on `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` to establish the very isomorphisms it's trying to use. The `\uses` declaration is incomplete—there's a missing gluing sub-lemma that would either directly identify `Γ(D(g_j), F)` with the localized sections (which creates the circularity) or provide an entirely different approach to avoid it.

Let me reconsider what `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` actually requires as input. The lemma needs both the localization property for the restricted map and identifications between the global sections on the distinguished open sets and the localizations of global sections—specifically, I need to show that `Γ(D(g_j), F)` identifies with `Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` and similarly for the product `fg_j`.

Let me reconsider whether there's another way to apply the span cover lemma. Looking back at the keystone proof statement, it seems to be claiming that when the lemma is applied to the globally presented sheaf `F_{(g_j)}` on `Spec R_{g_j}`, the section-restriction map at the image of `f` becomes a localization at powers of `f`, and under the section comparison this should give exactly the localized section map `(ρ_f)_{g_j}`.

The blueprint then applies the span cover descent lemma with the localized maps `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` as the input, using the fact that each localized map satisfies the localization property and the `g_j` generate the unit ideal to conclude that `ρ_f` itself is a localization.

But there's a gap in the proof: the claim that "under the section comparison this is exactly the statement that `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` is IsLocalizedModule(powers f)" isn't justified. The section comparison lemma only gives a sectionwise identification, not the R-module isomorphism `Γ(D(g_j), F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` that would be needed to make this claim work, and this isomorphism isn't established by any of the cited lemmas.

Looking back at the strategy document, there's actually a resolution candidate mentioned: reusing the exact sequence from `exact_of_isLocalized_span` or `sectionCech` on the cover, treating each `F_{(g_j)}` as tile-structured to establish the isomorphism through the tilde map. The strategy acknowledges this descent step exists, but the current proof hasn't implemented it. The sheaf-gluing and Čech cohomology content is what's actually needed here, but it's missing from the `\uses` list entirely—this is a critical gap that needs fixing. Now I'm moving on to audit the B3b and B4 blocks, starting with `lem:restrict_over_compat` which references the Lean declaration and pulls in several dependencies around module restrictions and pushforward equivalences. The statement's `\uses` block includes four dependencies, while the proof's `\uses` adds `lem:overEquivalence_isContinuous` as a fifth—this is acceptable since the extra dependency is only needed for the proof itself, not the statement. Now moving to B4...

Looking at the Lean definitions and their dependencies, I see the bundled helper functions are properly listed, and the `\uses` block correctly includes `def:modules_over_basicOpen_equivalence` as specified by the directive, along with the other required lemmas.

The proof structure flows through the inverse isomorphism, the compatibility bridge, the transport via `lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib`, and finally the affine transport along the scheme isomorphism D(g) ≅ Spec R_g. The B3c step adequately justifies that restriction preserves colimits and carries the structure-sheaf unit correctly because the identification is an isomorphism of schemes.

Let me reconsider whether there's a non-circular path here. The key question is whether `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` can be applied differently. If I set M = Γ(X, F) and N = Γ(D(f), F), then `(ρ_f)_{g_j}` is the localization of the restriction map at the element g_j. From the section localization lemma applied to F_{(g_j)}, I should be able to identify the source as sections over the localized spectrum and the target as sections over the original sheaf.

Using definitional equality of sections, the source becomes Γ(D(g_j), F) and the target becomes Γ(D(fg_j), F). So the restriction of the localization map at g_j is actually a map between these two spaces, which should satisfy the localization property for powers of f.

Now I need to check whether Γ(D(g_j), F) can be identified with M[g_j^{-1}] and Γ(D(fg_j), F) with N[g_j^{-1}], where M and N are the global sections. The issue is that for an arbitrary quasi-coherent sheaf, Γ(D(g_j), F) isn't automatically equal to the localization Γ(X, F)[g_j^{-1}].

The proof claims this identification follows from the comparison, but that seems circular. However, there's a crucial non-circularity argument in the keystone proof that uses the right-exactness of the associated sheaf functor to establish the identification properly. The identification between `Γ(D(g_j), F)` and `Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` is the crucial bridge needed to connect the section-level result on `Spec R_{g_j}` to the abstract localization of R-modules, and this step either creates a circularity or requires additional justification that isn't provided by the cited lemma.

Moving through the findings, I'm checking the other lemmas in the chapter. `lem:restrict_over_compat` is now solid after the recent edits—the statement properly aligns with the Lean definition and the `\uses` list is accurate. For `lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen`, the proof is complete and correct, with the Lean code now properly bundling the relevant definitions.

But I'm hitting a critical issue with the keystone lemma `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule`. The descent step in the proof is incomplete—it asserts that the localized section map satisfies the IsLocalizedModule property, but the argument relies on an isomorphism between global sections and localized sections that isn't properly established in the proof text.

The `\uses` list doesn't include any lemma that actually justifies this sheaf-gluing identification, and the STRATEGY.md itself flags this as a known open risk around descent non-circularity. This is a genuine gap that needs to be addressed before the proof can be considered sound.

`Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` is fully proved with all blocks marked either `\leanok` or `\mathlibok`. 6214	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
6215	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
6216	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
6217	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
6218	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
6219	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
6220	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
6221	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
6222	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
6223	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
6224	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
6225	  \[
6226	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
6227	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
6228	  \]
6229	\end{lemma}
6230	
6231	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
6232	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
6233	% the sheaf
6234	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
6235	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
6236	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
6237	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
6238	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
6239	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
6240	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
6241	% opens we win."
6242	\begin{proof}
6243	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
6244	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
6245	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
6246	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
6247	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
6248	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
6249	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
6250	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
6251	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
6252	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
6253	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
6254	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
6255	
6256	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
6257	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
6258	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
6259	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
6260	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
6261	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
6262	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
6263	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
6264	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
6265	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
6266	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
6267	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
6268	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
6269	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
6270	  \(k \geq 1\).
6271	\end{proof}
6272	
6273	\begin{lemma}
6274	
6275	\leanok
6276	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
6277	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
6278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
6279	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}

# Blueprint Review Report

## Slug
iter041

## Iteration
041

## Top-level summaries

### Proofs lacking detail

- `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` / `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule`: The descent step
  "under the section comparison this is exactly the statement that the gⱼ-localized section map
  (ρ_f)_{g_j} : Γ(X,F)_{g_j} → Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j} satisfies IsLocalizedModule(powers f)" is not
  supported by any cited lemma. `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` applied to F_{(g_j)}
  on Spec R_{g_j} gives that `Γ(D(g_j),F) → Γ(D(fg_j),F)` is IsLocalizedModule(powers f) (via
  `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib`'s definitional equality). But `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover`
  requires `(ρ_f)_{g_j} : Γ(X,F)_{g_j} → Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}` — a map between abstract R-module
  localizations, not between sections over opens. Bridging them requires `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}`
  (sheaf-gluing/Čech-H⁰), which is (a) the keystone for f replaced by g_j (circular) or (b) an
  unstated supporting sub-lemma. Neither `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib` nor anything else in the `\uses`
  list supplies this identification. STRATEGY.md § "KEYSTONE descent non-circularity (NEW, iter-041,
  load-bearing)" confirms the gap is live and unresolved.

### Dependency & isolation findings

- `lean_aux` node (no chapter, ID `lean:Alg…`): isolated, 0 edges, 0 impact. **Disposition: keep.**
  Uncovered Lean helper with no blueprint block; not a blueprint gap. No writer action needed.

## Per-chapter

### blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex — complete + correct, no notes.

### blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex — complete + correct, no notes.

### blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex

- **complete**: partial
- **correct**: partial
- **notes**:

  **B3b (`lem:restrict_over_compat`) — PASS after iter-041 edits.**
  Statement correctly describes `engine.inverse.obj(F.over D(g)) ≅ F.restrict ι` in
  D̂(g).Modules, matching the actual Lean decl `overBasicOpenIsoRestrict`. Stale
  `% NOTE: SCOPE MISMATCH` was removed. `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overBasicOpenIsoRestrict}` is
  accurate. `\uses` (statement: `def:modules_over_basicOpen_equivalence`,
  `lem:modules_restrict_basicOpen`, `lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib`,
  `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib`; proof adds `lem:overEquivalence_isContinuous`) is accurate. Proof
  prose covers B3a (structure-sheaf compatibility) and B3b (module equivalence on subspace) with
  adequate detail. B3c affine transport correctly deferred to B4. No issues.

  **B4 (`lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen`) — PASS after iter-041 edits.**
  `\lean{}` now bundles `presentationModulesRestrictBasicOpen`, `restrictBasicOpenUnitIso`,
  `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_isIso_basicOpen`. `\uses` gained `def:modules_over_basicOpen_equivalence`.
  Proof prose correctly chains: B2 (present F.over D(g_j)) → engine inverse → B3b bridge
  (`lem:restrict_over_compat`, `lem:presentation_ofIsIso_mathlib`) → B3c affine transport (D(g) ≅
  Spec R_g isomorphism, unit compatibility). No issues.

  **MUST-FIX: `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` — proof sketch has an unjustified descent step.**
  Statement, `\lean{}` hint, and overall route description are correct. The `\uses` block (statement
  and proof both list: `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover`, `lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_subcover`,
  `lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover`, `lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen`,
  `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation`) does not include any lemma establishing
  `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` as R-modules.

  The precise gap: `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` on F_{(g_j)} (globally presented
  on Spec R_{g_j}) gives `IsLocalizedModule(powers f) (Γ(D(g_j),F) → Γ(D(fg_j),F))` — via
  `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib`'s sectionwise definitional equality `Γ(F_{(g_j)}, V) = Γ(F, ι(V))`.
  But `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` (step B6) takes M = Γ(X,F), N = Γ(D(f),F), and
  requires `IsLocalizedModule(powers f) ((ρ_f)_{g_j} : M_{g_j} → N_{g_j})` — i.e., the
  LOCALIZED MAPS between abstract R-module localizations, not section maps between open-restriction
  groups. The proof text's phrase "under the section comparison this is exactly the statement …"
  asserts the identification `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` and `Γ(D(fg_j),F) ≅ Γ(D(f),F)_{g_j}`
  as a byproduct of `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib`, but `lem:restrict_obj_mathlib` only gives the
  definitional sectionwise equality; it does NOT give the abstract module localization
  identification.

  To close the gap, the blueprint needs either:
  (a) A new sub-lemma `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` proved non-circularly before invoking the
      descent — STRATEGY.md proposes reusing P3 `exact_of_isLocalized_span` / sectionCech H⁰ on
      the cover (with each F_{(g_j)} tile-structured via B4 ⟹ IsIso fromTildeΓ ⟹ tilde), or
  (b) A redesigned proof that feeds `lem:isLocalizedModule_of_span_cover` with inputs that match
      what `lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation` actually delivers.

  **The chapter is `correct: partial` because `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule` has a proof gap
  at the descent step. HARD GATE for keystone dispatch does NOT clear.**

  **Informational: 3 blueprint nodes missing `\lean{}` hints.**
  - `lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy` (line 2238): NOTE in chapter explains the contracting
    homotopy is obtained by transporting `cechEnginePrepend` across the degreewise iso, so no
    standalone Lean decl exists; intentional.
  - `lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy_spec` (line ~2302): same situation (companion spec block).
  - One additional block starting with `lem:isIso_f…` — appears to be a to-build block awaiting a
    `\lean{}` annotation; not on an active prover route this iter.
  These are informational only; add `\lean{}` pins when the corresponding Lean declarations are named.

## Severity summary

**must-fix-this-iter**:
1. `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` / `lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule`: proof sketch is
   incomplete — the descent step "under the section comparison …" is unjustified; a sheaf-gluing /
   Čech-H⁰ sub-lemma establishing `Γ(D(g_j),F) ≅ Γ(X,F)_{g_j}` as R-modules is missing from both
   the proof text and the `\uses` block. Chapter is `correct: partial`. **HARD GATE for keystone
   dispatch does NOT clear.** Dispatch the blueprint-writer for `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex`
   with a directive targeting exactly this descent step, following the STRATEGY.md resolution
   candidate (P3 `exact_of_isLocalized_span` / sectionCech H⁰ route with tile-structured F_{(g_j)}).

**informational**:
- 3 blueprint nodes in `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` missing `\lean{}` hints
  (`lem:cech_free_eval_prepend_homotopy`, its spec companion, and one `lem:isIso_f…` block). Two
  are intentional per in-chapter NOTE; add `\lean{}` pins for the third when the decl name is known.

Overall verdict: `Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage.tex` is `correct: partial` — the keystone proof
sketch hides an unjustified descent step that requires a sheaf-gluing/Čech-H⁰ sub-lemma not present
in the chapter; the HARD GATE does NOT clear for keystone dispatch this iter. The two edited blocks
(B3b, B4) pass cleanly. The other two chapters (`Cohomology_HigherDirectImage.tex`,
`Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex`) are complete and correct. All 3 phases in the strategy have
adequate blueprint coverage — no unstarted-phase proposals.
